Fasd Audiovisual Record of Interview (avroi) Guide
sections.h:
DEFINITIONS

Foetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders (FASD): Is a non-diagnostic umbrella term that is used to cover the full range of possible birth defects and developmental issues that can be caused by prenatal exposure to alcohol. The term reflects the broad range of physical, cognitive, developmental and behavioural effects that prenatal alcohol exposure can have. It is a lifelong disorder, often with no physical abnormalities despite the sufferer being profoundly affected.1

Vulnerable Person: For the purpose of a police interview, be that suspect or witness, is defined as someone who falls within one or more of the following categories:

• A child is always a vulnerable person.

• People with impaired intellectual function are always vulnerable.

Or people who:

• Have impaired physical functioning.

• Are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islanders.

• Are from non-English speaking backgrounds.

• Have suffered an emotional or traumatic incident.

Which as a result, may:

• Predispose them to suggestibility.

• Impact on voluntariness.

• Complicate communications between the police and the interviewee.

Any of the above circumstances may result in flawed information being captured during the interview.

Persons fitting this definition must be afforded enhanced services to ensure fairness to the person and accuracy of the information obtained during an interview.

Child: The term child means any boy or girl under the age of 18 years and in the absence of positive evidence as to age, any boy or girl apparently under the age of 18 years.2

Responsible Adult: In relation to a young person, means a parent, guardian, or other person having responsibility for the day to day care of the young person but does not include a person who the regulations may provide is not a responsible adult.3 Independent Person: An adult, other than a member of the police, who is present with the consent of the person responsible for the young person (if the responsible person can be found)

• In the case of a young person who is of or above the age of 16 years, an adult

who was present at the consent of the young person;

• A legal practitioner of the young person's own choosing (if the young person is

over 16 years of age); or

1 Australian Government, Australian Institute of Family Studies (2014).

2 The Criminal Code S.1

3 Young Offenders Act s 20

• A person who has not been involved in the investigation of the offence and has

no interest in the outcome of the investigation and who is present during the interview and who is approved by the child.

Police officers should endeavor to identify responsible persons within their district who may be used as independent persons when required. Where practicable, persons of the same sex and similar ethnic, cultural and/or religious background to the young person should be utilised.

NB: To protect their independent judicial status, Justices of the Peace will not be used.

A member of the WA Police Force (sworn or unsworn) cannot be used as an independent person during an interview, unless the member is the parent or guardian of the young person, or is nominated by the parent or guardian of the young person.4 Culturally and Linguistically Diverse (CALD): A person who is vulnerable due to their culture or whose second language is English.5

PURPOSE

This guide assists interviewers when they are required to interview an individual who is suspected of, or confirmed as having FASD. Individuals suffering from FASD have developmental impairments and are considered vulnerable requiring additional support.

Each individual will be different and it is the responsibility of the interviewing officer to apply the information within the guide appropriately.

Although this guide is primarily for suspects suffering with FASD, the learning may be applied to all aspects of communication.

sections.h:
Fundamental to all interviews conducted by WA Police are the principles of Voluntariness and Fairness. In all instances, the interviewing officer must satisfy themselves the suspect understands the interview process, their legislative, case law and procedural rights and the caution. One of the ways this can be achieved is by asking questions of the interviewee to assess their understanding.

When interviewing an adult suspect with FASD, an Interview Friend should be considered. Ultimately, the use of the interview friend will be a decision for the interviewee, but one should always be offered.

When interviewing a child suspect with FASD, a Responsible Adult or an independent person must be present. In addition to the presence of a Responsible Adult, an Interview Friend may also be present if the child requests one, or if the interviewing officer believes an Interview Friend would be beneficial to the interview process.

The interviewing officer must be satisfied that the interviewee understands the interview process, their legislative, case law and procedural rights including the caution. If the interviewing officer is not satisfied that the interviewee understands any of these matters, they should in consultation with a supervisor, decide whether the person is to be interviewed.

sections.h:
FASD6 is a mental impairment and will effect individuals differently depending on the severity of the condition. The neurological effects of FASD can have some cognitive and behavioural consequences such as; learning disabilities, poor memory, hyperactivity and in some cases, low IQ. These neurological effects may increase the likelihood of suggestibility in individuals with FASD; a term referring to the ability for an individual to be easily persuaded to take another’s position.

This increased vulnerability of suggestibility for individuals with FASD requires careful consideration when interviewing a suspect with FASD, to ensure the interview is conducted with the principles of fairness and voluntariness. In addition to applying the tips outlined below, all suspect interviews will be conducted using the Investigative Interviewing, Conversation Management model.

Individuals with suspected or confirmed FASD, function highest in structured and predictable environments. At the beginning of the interview, it may be helpful to explain the format of the interview and the type of questions that will be asked in detail, to decrease the likelihood of adverse emotional and behavioural reactions. In addition to this, individuals with FASD may have sensory processing disturbances that make crowds, noise, or bright lights difficult to manage. These conditions can cause agitation 6 s6(1) Mental Health Act 2014

and difficulty to focus. Therefore, it may be helpful to conduct the interview in a quiet and softly lit environment with limited persons present in the room.

An adult with FASD frequently presents with the same development and functioning capabilities of half their chronological age (dysmaturity). This factor should be taken into account and reflected in the simple language used by the interviewer. This is especially important when ascertaining if the individual can display a level of understanding of their legislative rights and the caution.

In accordance with the Investigative Interviewing and Conversation Management Model, interviewers should provide the individual with an opportunity to tell their version of the story, uninterrupted at the outset of the interview session. Interviewers should rely on open questions (TEDS, 5W&H) and check often for a level of understanding.

Interviewers should also only ask one question at a time and use simple language, as those with FASD have significant difficulty understanding abstract concepts and making inferences from context. Multiple questions asked in the one sentence should be avoided.

Interviewers should refrain from using judgmental or accusatory tones or phrases because this can heighten anxiety and limit information processing in individuals with FASD. Similarly, using overly complimentary language may encourage the individual to falsely answer questions, in order to please authority. In all instances, seek corroborative evidence to confirm the suspect’s version of events.

If individuals with FASD are struggling to give verbal responses, allow them to communicate in a different way. If appropriate, consider allowing them to act it out or draw pictures to assist their response. Alternatively, consider writing out the questions and allow them to write out their response.

Suggestibility coupled with memory deficiencies may lead to confabulation.

Confabulation is an unintentional effort to fill in gaps of memory, without the intention to be untruthful or mislead others7. Suggestion and confabulation are particularly likely in situations where interviewers ask leading questions or pressure interviewees.

Interviewers should only challenge a suspect’s version of events in the ‘Challenge Phase’ of an interview, in line with the Conversation Management Model.

If reading is required in the interview, allow adequate reading time for individuals with FASD and offer the option of reading the material aloud. Be sure to verify comprehension as some individuals with FASD develop strategies such as displaying strong emotional reactions, to avoid detection of their poor reading skills, even if they agreed to read the material.

Individuals with FASD generally work harder to focus on social interactions, so they may only be able to handle a few minutes of an interview at a time. If pushed too hard, they may become withdrawn (shut down or not speak at all) or volatile (overly emotional, crying, screaming or violent towards self and others). In this emotional state, there is risk the individual may provide false or inaccurate information as a strategy to 7 Foetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder and Suggestibility/Brown et al. British Journal of Clinical Psychology (1993) cope with the inability to handle the anxious situation. In line with Investigative Interviewing principles, taking breaks in the case of interviewing someone with FASD, may increase the likelihood of gaining more accurate information. A break could include suspending the interview and allowing the individual to leave the room for a period of time, if practicable.

Many people with FASD find their brains work better when their bodies are engaged in some form of movement. It may be helpful if the individual engages in some form of physical activity such as; pacing about the room, doodling, fidgeting with a stress ball or other small benign item during the interview, if safe to do so.

FASD and suggestibility may increase the likelihood of false confessions during police interviews. This increases the risk of interviews deemed inadmissible if vulnerabilities are not adequately identified and addressed. By following these tips outlined above and increasing awareness about FASD, these risks may be decreased.8

Individuals with suspected or confirmed FASD, function highest in structured and predictable environments. At the beginning of the interview, it may be helpful to explain the format of the interview and the type of questions that will be asked in detail, to decrease the likelihood of adverse emotional and behavioural reactions. In addition to this, individuals with FASD may have sensory processing disturbances that make crowds, noise, or bright lights difficult to manage. These conditions can cause agitation 6 s6(1) Mental Health Act 2014 and difficulty to focus. Therefore, it may be helpful to conduct the interview in a quiet and softly lit environment with limited persons present in the room. An adult with FASD frequently presents with the same development and functioning capabilities of half their chronological age (dysmaturity). This factor should be taken into account and reflected in the simple language used by the interviewer. This is especially important when ascertaining if the individual can display a level of understanding of their legislative rights and the caution. In accordance with the Investigative Interviewing and Conversation Management Model, interviewers should provide the individual with an opportunity to tell their version of the story, uninterrupted at the outset of the interview session. Interviewers should rely on open questions (TEDS, 5W&H) and check often for a level of understanding. Interviewers should also only ask one question at a time and use simple language, as those with FASD have significant difficulty understanding abstract concepts and making inferences from context. Multiple questions asked in the one sentence should be avoided. Interviewers should refrain from using judgmental or accusatory tones or phrases because this can heighten anxiety and limit information processing in individuals with FASD. Similarly, using overly complimentary language may encourage the individual to falsely answer questions, in order to please authority. In all instances, seek corroborative evidence to confirm the suspect’s version of events. If individuals with FASD are struggling to give verbal responses, allow them to communicate in a different way. If appropriate, consider allowing them to act it out or draw pictures to assist their response. Alternatively, consider writing out the questions and allow them to write out their response. Suggestibility coupled with memory deficiencies may lead to confabulation. Confabulation is an unintentional effort to fill in gaps of memory, without the intention to be untruthful or mislead others7. Suggestion and confabulation are particularly likely in situations where interviewers ask leading questions or pressure interviewees. Interviewers should only challenge a suspect’s version of events in the ‘Challenge Phase’ of an interview, in line with the Conversation Management Model. If reading is required in the interview, allow adequate reading time for individuals with FASD and offer the option of reading the material aloud. Be sure to verify comprehension as some individuals with FASD develop strategies such as displaying strong emotional reactions, to avoid detection of their poor reading skills, even if they agreed to read the material. Individuals with FASD generally work harder to focus on social interactions, so they may only be able to handle a few minutes of an interview at a time. If pushed too hard, they may become withdrawn (shut down or not speak at all) or volatile (overly emotional, crying, screaming or violent towards self and others). In this emotional state, there is risk the individual may provide false or inaccurate information as a strategy to 7 Foetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder and Suggestibility/Brown et al. British Journal of Clinical Psychology (1993) cope with the inability to handle the anxious situation. In line with Investigative Interviewing principles, taking breaks in the case of interviewing someone with FASD, may increase the likelihood of gaining more accurate information. A break could include suspending the interview and allowing the individual to leave the room for a period of time, if practicable. Many people with FASD find their brains work better when their bodies are engaged in some form of movement. It may be helpful if the individual engages in some form of physical activity such as; pacing about the room, doodling, fidgeting with a stress ball or other small benign item during the interview, if safe to do so. FASD and suggestibility may increase the likelihood of false confessions during police interviews. This increases the risk of interviews deemed inadmissible if vulnerabilities are not adequately identified and addressed. By following these tips outlined above and increasing awareness about FASD, these risks may be decreased.8

sections.h:
Sound judgement should be used when applying the Anunga Guidelines, for instance;

it may not be necessary to provide the interviewee every Anunga rule on every occasion. A scaled approach reduces the likelihood of appearing condescending and thus increases rapport.

Remember, Anunga Guidelines9 are about fairness and voluntariness. It is your responsibility to ensure that their application achieves those two requirements.

There are nine Anunga rules (in italics), each followed with some explanatory notes.

Unless fluent in English provide an interpreter

1) When an aboriginal person is being interrogated as a suspect, unless he is as

fluent in English as the average white man of English descent, an interpreter able to interpret in and from the aboriginal person's language should be present, and his assistance should be utilised whenever necessary to ensure complete and mutual understanding.

If the suspect is communicating with a basic level of understanding of the English language, it is likely they may not fully comprehend instructions or questions asked of them. In all instances, you must use the services of a qualified interpreter. Further, it may become apparent the suspect’s cognitive understanding is still questionable, despite having the assistance of an interpreter. In this instance, consult with your supervisor and decide whether to continue with the interview.

During the interview speak directly to the suspect or witness using short simple phrases. This improves the interpreter’s ability to interpret the exact words.

8 Foetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder and Suggestibility/Brown et al. British Journal of Clinical Psychology (1993) 9 R v Anunga (1976) 11 ALR 412

Give the option of an Interview Friend

2) When an Aboriginal is being interrogated it is desirable where practicable that

a "prisoner's friend"10 (who may also be the interpreter) be present. The "prisoner's friend', should be someone in whom the Aboriginal has apparent confidence. He may be a mission or settlement superintendent or a member of the staff of one of these institutions who knows and is known to the Aboriginal. He may be a station owner, manager or overseer or an officer from the Department of Aboriginal Affairs. The combinations of persons and situations are variable and the categories of persons I have mentioned are not exclusive. The important thing is that the "prisoner's friend', be someone in whom the Aboriginal has confidence, by whom he will feel supported.

An interview friend is a person who is present at the request of the suspect. They must be someone known to the suspect, independent of the police, able to speak the suspect's principal language and be someone the interviewee has confidence in. The interview friend must also be impartial to the matter under investigation.

If you cannot locate an interview friend, you should consider using the services of an independent person or decide not to proceed with the interview. Even though there is no statutory obligation to provide a witness with an interview friend, an interview friend may be present for the purposes of assisting in the interview process.

Ensure Caution is given and all elements are understood

3) Great care should be taken in administering the caution when it is appropriate

to do so. It is simply not adequate to administer it in the usual terms and say, "Do you understand that?" or "Do you understand you do not have to answer questions?" Interrogating Police Officers, having explained the caution in simple terms, should ask the Aboriginal to tell them what is meant by the caution, phrase by phrase, and should not proceed with the interrogation until it is clear the Aboriginal has apparent understanding of his right to remain silent. Most experienced police officers in the Territory already do this. The problem of the caution is a difficult one but the presence of a "prisoner's friend"

or interpreter and adequate and simple questioning about the caution should go a long way towards solving it.

A simple yes or no answer from the suspect does not demonstrate their understanding.

The caution may need to be broken down into manageable sections. The police caution consists of three key parts. It is the interviewing officer’s responsibility to ensure the suspect’s explanation of all parts demonstrates their full understanding of the caution.

If, after this process, you do not feel the suspect understands the caution you should stop the interview and seek advice from a supervisor.

10 Western Australia Police Force use the term Interview Friend Formulation of questions

4) Great care should be taken in formulating questions so that so far as possible

the answer which is wanted or expected is not suggested in any way.

Anything in the nature of cross-examination should be scrupulously avoided, as answers to it have no probative value. It should be borne in mind that it is not only the wording of the question which may suggest the answer but also the manner and tone of voice which are used.

Officers should use TEDS (Tell, Explain, Describe, Show) and 5W&H (Who, What, When, Where, Why and How) style questions to obtain open responses from the suspect. Take time to formulate questions during the interview and don’t rush. Keep your questions short and simple, be patient and allow sufficient time to answer. If the suspect does not understand your question, re-phrase it.

Corroborate any admissions from other sources

5) Even when an apparent frank and free confession has been obtained relating

to the commission of an offence, police should continue to investigate the matter in an endeavour to obtain proof of the commission of the offence from other sources. Failure to do this, among other things, led to the rejection of confessional records of interview in the cases of Nari WHEELER and Frank

JAGAMARA.

Always seek to clarify any admissions made by gathering further evidence to support the information given by the suspect. Due to their culture, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander people who are suspects may want to please the interviewer and inadvertently admit to something they have little or no knowledge of.

Offer meals, drinks, have toilets available to use

6) Because Aboriginal people are often nervous and ill at ease in the presence of

white authority figures like policemen, it is particularly important that they be offered a meal, if they are being interviewed in a Police Station or in the company of Police or in custody when a meal time arrives. They should always be offered a drink of water. They should be asked if they wish to use the lavatory if they are in the company of Police or under arrest.

A meal, drink and an offer to use the toilet should be offered to the suspect. The offer of a meal and drink is not necessarily about sustenance but about making the interviewee feel comfortable. As stated earlier, the Anunga Guidelines are applied in a scaled approach, for example, if an interviewee has only just had a meal it may not be necessary to offer one, however that should be addressed during the introduction phase of the interview.

Should not interview anyone who is ill, intoxicated or tired

7) It is particularly important that Aboriginal and other people are not interrogated

when they are disabled by illness or drunkenness or tiredness. Admissions so gained will probably be rejected by a Court. Interrogation should not continue for an unreasonably long time.

If a suspect indicates or you believe they are tired, then the interview will be delayed until they are rested. If the suspect says they are tired and wishes to rest you must facilitate this.

If a suspect presents or indicates as being intoxicated, drug affected or ill you will not interview them. You will allow them to rest/sleep and only interview them once they are in a fit state. Section 141(k) CIA gives the power to detain a suspect for this purpose.

If the interview is lengthy, the suspect requests it or you believe the suspect would benefit from a break, then a break should be considered.

Legal assistance is offered and reasonable steps taken to obtain it

8) Should an Aboriginal seek legal assistance reasonable steps should be taken

to obtain such assistance. If an Aboriginal states he does not wish to answer further questions or any questions the interrogation should not continue.

If a suspect requests to speak with a legal practitioner, this is to be allowed either in person or on the telephone. This right is usually exercised before the interview however, the right may be exercised during or after the interview. Talking with a legal practitioner is an ongoing right of a suspect and if during the interview, they request to speak with a legal practitioner you must stop the interview and facilitate the request.

If the suspect indicates they do not want to answer any questions the interview should be stopped immediately, however, the interviewer needs to be mindful of whether the interviewee is merely uncomfortable with that particular topic or whether they are declining to answer any questions.

If clothing is seized/removed, provide substitute clothing

9) When it is necessary to remove clothing for forensic examination or for the

purposes of medical examination, steps must be taken forthwith to supply substitute clothing.

Be mindful that wearing the standard forensic overalls police provide to suspects when there clothing has been seized may be perceived as prejudicial to the suspect if your AVROI is viewed by a magistrate, jury or judge. If practicable, provide them with everyday replacement clothing.

CONCLUSION

Interviewing an individual with a vulnerability is challenging. Interviewing officers must be vigilant and where possible identify any vulnerabilities prior to interviewing and implement the required supports.

Interviewers must endeavor to ensure they comply with the principles of voluntariness and fairness at all times.

To Responsible Adult: Your role is to ensure that the suspect is treated fairly and to assist him/her in understanding the proceedings, rights, charge/s, any questions asked by the police and privacy if required.

If at any time during the interview you feel it is unfair please immediately bring this to the interviewing officer’s attention. However, you are not here to ask your own questions or answer questions on behalf of the suspect.

During this interview, you should not attempt to influence any response from the suspect. They are not obliged to speak with Police if they do not want to. sections.h:
Ensure Caution is given and all elements are understood. Care must be taken to ensure the suspect understands.

It is your responsibility to ensure the suspect understands their right to silence and any evidence provided by the suspect can be used in court proceedings.

The caution is to be delivered in a manner and language in which the suspect understands. For example, the interviewing officer does not have to use the same words as the caution i.e., ‘obliged’ can be changed to ‘don’t’. The best way to show if the suspect understands is to ask a series of questions about each section of the caution.

You do not have to say anything unless you wish to do so anything you say or do will be recorded and

may be used in evidence.

You do not have to answer my questions if you don’t want to. – “Tell me what this means”

It is the interviewing officer’s responsibility to ensure the suspect understands their ‘right to silence’. Use open style questions to encourage the suspect to speak and provide a detailed answer. Questions could be phrased similar to the below:

• Whose choice is it to answer questions?

• How many questions do you have to answer?

• Can you speak or be silent? Why?

Do not accept ‘yes’ or ‘no’ answers as they do not demonstrate understanding.

Anything you say or do will be recorded - “Tell me what this means”

The interviewing officer’s must ensure the suspect understands if they do not exercise their ‘right to silence,’ their answers will be recorded. Use open style questions to encourage the suspect to speak and provide a detailed answer. Questions could be phrased similar to the below:

• How is this interview being recorded?

• What will happen if you answer questions?

Do not accept ‘yes’ or ‘no’ answers as they do not demonstrate understanding.

And may be used in evidence - “Tell me what this means”

The interviewing officer’s must ensure the suspect understands, that their recorded answers may be used as evidence in court. Use open style questions to encourage the suspect to speak and provide a detailed answer. Questions could be phrased similar to the below:

• Where can this interview be played?

• Who will hear the interview?

Do not accept ‘yes’ or ‘no’ answers as they do not demonstrate understanding.

NOTE: It is the interviewing officer’s responsibility to ask questions of the suspect to ensure their understanding of the interview process and their legislative, case law and procedural rights.

INTRODUCTION

This manual has been created to provide police officers with an easy to read guide to investigative interviewing of witnesses.

The manual deals with interviewing witnesses of all categories. It is important for the interviewer to identify into which category each witness falls, as not all witnesses should be dealt with in the same manner.

An eyewitness is someone who personally saw or was involved in the incident under investigation. A non-eyewitness, which includes, other significant witness, general witness or an expert witness are witnesses who may not have seen the incident occur but have other information relevant to the inquiry. In all cases the overall purpose of the interview is the same, although the interviewers approach will be slightly different.

Practice in using the Free Recall Model will enhance the natural flexibility of the model, resulting in a free flowing interview style that meets the interviewer’s objective.

Related Information

More information on investigative interviewing can be found on the Investigative Standards Intranet Site (Investigative Interviewing page).

DEFINITIONS

To assist officers, a definition of terms used in this manual is shown below.

ADVOKATE Is the mnemonic for:

A – Amount of time under observation.

D – Distance from the witnessed event.

V – Visibility at the time of the event.

O – Obstructions.

K – Known or seen before.

A – Any reason to remember.

T – Time since the incident.

E – Errors or discrepancies.

This mnemonic is specifically for police to capture evidence that fall within each of the above headings when taking statements or conducting interviews.

Automated Interview Plan (AIP): Electronic interview plan used for suspects and witnesses. Located on the WA Police Force Intranet here .

Challenge: Seeking an explanation from the interviewee for inconsistencies between facts known by police and the interviewees account.

Cognitive Interview Model: The cognitive interview is a memory facilitator, with the interviewee being in control of the information flowing from the interview.

Culturally and Linguistically Diverse (CaLD): A person who is vulnerable due to their culture or whose second language is English.

Free Recall Model: A basic interview model which can be used for interviewing witnesses in the majority of situations.

Interviewee: The person who is being interviewed.

Investigating Officer (IO): The officer who is investigating the incident.

Investigative interviewing: The questioning of witnesses and suspects to obtain accurate and reliable information about matters under investigation.

Investigatively Important Topics: Of value to the investigation because it helps to establish the nature and elements of the offence, possible defences, the identity of the offender or further lines of enquiry.

PEACE Model: An interviewing framework that provides a structure to planning and conducting effective investigative interviews.

Physical Material (PM): Items which include DNA, fingerprints, impressions, photographs, clothing, weapons, documents, computer generated data, trace evidence, Closed Circuit Television (CCTV) footage, ballistics, crash reports and all other forensic samples.

Suspect: A person suspected of having committed an offence, whether or not he or she has been charged with the offence.

TEDS: A mnemonic that is helpful when constructing open questions. It stands for: Tell, Explain, Describe, Show.

Victim: A person injured, or suffering loss or harm as a result of an offence.

Vulnerable Person: For the purpose of a police interview, be that suspect or witness, is defined as someone who falls within one or more of the following categories:

• A child is always a vulnerable person.

• People with impaired intellectual function are always vulnerable.

Or people who:

• Have impaired physical functioning.

• Are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islanders.

• Are from non-English speaking backgrounds.

• Have suffered an emotional or traumatic incident.

Which as a result, may:

• Predispose them to suggestibility.

• Impact on voluntariness.

• Complicate communications between the police and the interviewee.

Any of the above circumstances may result in flawed information being captured during the interview.

Persons fitting this definition must be afforded enhanced services to ensure fairness to the person and accuracy of the information obtained during an interview.

Witness: A person who has information about an alleged offence or offender, even if they were not physically present at the event. Everyone who provides relevant information is a 'witness' so the term includes victim, eyewitness, other significant witness, general witness, expert witness or police witness.

5W&H: A mnemonic that is helpful when constructing open questions. It stands for: Who, What, When, Where, Why and How.

sections.h:
Non-Eyewitness

Some confusion has arisen in the past in relation to non-eyewitness interview techniques.

This was largely due to the failure of interviewers to recognise different approaches are required for eyewitness vs non-eyewitnesses. As stated in the introduction to this manual, a non-eyewitness is someone who has not actually witnessed the event but may be able to supply information relating to some other aspect of the matter. A non-eyewitness could be an expert witness providing technical evidence (For example, a bank teller explaining how credit card transactions are processed in a bank). An eyewitness will often be asked to recall the incident as though they were still at the scene but obviously this is not appropriate for a person who is not an eyewitness.

Who Is a Witness?

The WA Police Investigation Doctrine (the Doctrine) categorises witnesses into five categories:

Victim

• The person or entity upon which the offence was committed.

• Victimology.

• Proof of facts such as legal entity, age, legal status.

Eyewitness

• Directly observed the offence.

Other significant witness

• Observed an event prior to or post the offence which is classified as relevant

evidence.

General witness

• Provides relevant evidence such as continuity and/or procedure.

• Provides evidence of lead up events or background information.

Expert witness

• Provides evidence of a specialist nature and is permitted by the courts to express

an expert opinion.

sections.h:
Planning and Preparation: Review available information and establish interview aims and objectives. Identify if the witness is vulnerable and prepare and develop your plan. For example, during the planning phase the need to consider Anunga Guidelines or a responsible adult is identified.

Engage and Explain: Develop rapport, explain the interview processes and cover legislative obligations.

Account, Clarify and Challenge: Using the Free Recall or Cognitive Interview Model gain an account of events, clarify and challenge any inconsistencies. Account for new information.

Closure: Conclude the interview and address any concerns and future ground i.e. Court process, giving evidence etc.

Evaluation: Evaluate how the information obtained impacts on the investigation and also evaluate the performance of the interviewer.

There are two models utilised within the PEACE framework, for witnesses, they are the Free Recall Model and the Cognitive Interviewing Model.

The Free Recall Model is the model generally utilised to interview witnesses. Tier two trained interviewers may utilise the Cognitive Interviewing Model if the appropriate circumstances exist. The steps to conduct a Free Recall interview are outlined below. The Free Recall and Cognitive Interview Models will be covered fully later in this chapter.

Six Step Process:

Step 1 Set the scene and initiate a free recall using an open question.

Step 2 Second recall if required. Obtain sketch from witness if applicable.

Step 3 Identify interviewee topics.

Step 4 Expand on topics.

Step 5 Identify and expand on Police Agenda topics.

Step 6 Summarise, Clarify and prepare a statement.

This section provides guidelines and procedures for:

• Interviewing witnesses.

• Identifying and interviewing vulnerable witnesses.

It is designed to assist interviewers at all levels of Investigative Interviewing.

Why Interview Witnesses?

The purpose of interviewing witnesses is to ascertain the witness's account of the alleged event(s) and any other information that may assist the investigation.

Treat all Witnesses as Individuals

As an investigator, interviewing witnesses is part of your everyday business. Witnesses come from a wide range of backgrounds and each presents you with different challenges.

They all have different needs and concerns and levels of involvement in the investigation.

(For example, you may have a witness who is a victim of the crime or a witness with no links to the victim whatsoever but who saw what occurred). There can never be a ‘one size fits all’ approach to the interaction with witnesses and individual circumstances should always be taken into consideration.

Give Special Care to Victims

Treat all witnesses with fairness, empathy and sensitivity. As victims may be at a high risk of being traumatised, stressed and in fear, additional care should be taken when conducting an interview with them. It is likely the recollection of events may be traumatic and their behaviour should be monitored. Their behaviour and ability could also be impacted by physical injuries, intoxication, tiredness and so on (delay the interview if appropriate).

Consider whether the witness is currently in a safe environment and the type of information the witness is likely to give.

It is paramount you consider the witness's well-being and your investigative needs in this decision making.

sections.h:
Definition of a Vulnerable Person

A vulnerable person for the purpose of a police interview, be that suspect or witness, is defined as someone who falls within one or more of the following categories:

• A child is always a vulnerable person.

• People with impaired intellectual function are always vulnerable.

Or people who:

• Have impaired physical functioning.

• Are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islanders.

• Are from non-English speaking backgrounds.

• Have suffered an emotional or traumatic incident.

Which as a result, may:

• Predispose them to suggestibility.

• Impact on voluntariness.

• Complicate communications between the police and the interviewee.

Any of the above circumstances may result in flawed information being captured during the interview.

Persons fitting this definition must be afforded enhanced services to ensure fairness to the person and accuracy of the information obtained during an interview.

The following are important considerations that will impact on what provisions need to be made relating to witnesses. Decisions on who will conduct the interview, the structure, style, duration, and pace of the interview will be made based on information relevant to these issues. As the investigating officer (IO) it is paramount any factors effecting the vulnerability of a witness are identified and dealt with in line with legislation, policy and case law. If witnesses are not interviewed appropriately vital information may be missed, contaminated or not accepted by the court.

While it is not possible to supply an exhaustive list of people who may be vulnerable in the criminal justice system, the following is a list to assist in identifying vulnerability factors:

Child / juvenile.

Aboriginal people and Torres Strait Islander People.

Immaturity, either in terms of age or development.

Any infirmity, including early dementia or disease.

Mental illness.

Intellectual disability.

Illiteracy or limited education which may impair a person’s capacity to understand police questions.

People who are of non-English speaking background.

Alcoholism.

Physical disabilities including deafness or loss of sight.

Drug dependence.

Cultural, ethnic or religious factors including those relating to gender attitudes.

Trauma.

It must be understood a person’s vulnerability does not have to be permanent. Witnesses need to be assessed in accordance with how they present to police at the time of contact and should be supported appropriately.

Vulnerable Witnesses and Interview Considerations

Children

Definition of a child: A person who is under 18 years of age.

All children are vulnerable. The below table identifies additional vulnerable characteristics.

Children and those with a mental impairment who are victims or witnesses to a criminal offence should be interviewed by a Specialist Investigative Interviewer (SII). This interview is audio-visually recorded and can be played as their evidence-in-chief in court, refer section 106HA of the Evidence Act 1906. These interviews are only admissible when conducted by specially trained interviewers. The Child Assessment & Interview Team (CAIT) co-ordinate these interviews and, in conjunction with Detective Training School, deliver the SII training.

CAIT Request for Interview.

A child who is Culturally and Linguistically Diverse (CaLD) or English is their second language. An interpreter may be required (See below hyperlinks).

A child who has a physical impairment (for example, deafness, blindness etc.) that impedes their ability to understand and communicate.

A child who is Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander shall be interviewed in compliance with the Anunga Guidelines. An interpreter may be required (see below hyperlinks) if the police, responsible adult or the child identify one is necessary.

A child suffering trauma.

A child suffering a mental impairment.

Adult Witness of Aboriginal or Torres Strait Island Descent If the witness is an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander, the witness shall be interviewed in compliance with the Anunga Guidelines. An interpreter may be required (See below hyperlink) if the police or the witness identify one is necessary. An interview friend may also be required as outlined in the Anunga Guidelines.

A Witness Who is (CaLD) or English is Their Second Language To interview a person (adult or child) who does not understand, speak or communicate in English as a first language the interviewing officer may utilise an approved interpreter or translator as outlined in the Police Manual. (Refer to Working with Interpreters).

A Witness Suffering Mental Impairment or Disability

Prior to conducting an interview with a witness with a mental impairment or a disability, the interviewing officer should assess the ability of the witness to effectively understand and participate in the interview process. If the interviewing officer identifies that the witness has no, or limited understanding of the interview process, consideration should be given for a SCI to conduct the interview. (See CAIT information under Children heading).

If the witness has a disability, consideration should be given to utilising an interpreter or a translator if it is considered one is required. (Refer to Working with Interpreters) In all other cases when interviewing a witness who is vulnerable, the principles of the Anunga Guidelines should be considered.

Multiple Witnesses

If there is more than one witness to an incident care should be taken to minimise the risk of memory contamination between witnesses by:

• Separating the witnesses as soon as possible and interviewing them individually.

• If the interview is being delayed, consider asking the witnesses not to discuss the

incident with other people and explaining to them why you are taking this action, i.e.

the potential for post-event information to contaminate memory.

Witnesses Who Become Suspects

If a witness becomes a suspect during an interview, procedures for interviewing suspects should be adopted as soon as the suspicion arises. Do not continue interviewing the person as a witness in the hope of eliciting admissions or confessional material. Comply with all legal, policy and case law obligations. (Refer to Investigative Interviewing Suspect Manual).

If you are going to interview a person as a witness in relation to an incident and you suspect them in anyway of being involved in the offence as an offender, you must interview them as a suspect. (Refer to Investigative Interviewing Suspect Manual).

sections.t: Planning and Preparation for A Witness
sections.h:
INTERVIEW

Free Recall/Cognitive Interview Models

Planning and Preparation is one of the most important phases in effective interviewing.

Always plan and prepare a written interview plan, no matter what type of interview is being considered, to ensure you are ready to conduct an effective and ethical interview.

Officers are encouraged to use the Automated Interview Plan (AIP) when interviewing witnesses. It allows the interviewer to produce an interview plan tailored to the witness’s vulnerabilities.

What should be covered when planning and preparing for an interview?

Consider these factors when planning and preparing for an interview:

• Interview aims and objectives.

• Investigatively important topics.

• Witness profile: identity, criminal history, victimology.

Step 1 • Legal requirements.

• Interview structure.

• Contingencies for interviewee reaction.

• Practical arrangements, including whether or not the interview should be

delayed due to your witness being traumatised, intoxicated, etc.

Decide if the witness is vulnerable. Refer to the section in this manual relating Step 2

to interviewing vulnerable witnesses.

Step 3 Complete a written interview plan.

Interview Aims and Objectives

Develop a good understanding of the investigation and the purpose of the interview by:

• Examining all available, relevant information, witness statements, scene

examination, physical material (PM) and other supporting documents.

• Setting the aims and objectives for the interview. These should focus on the

investigatively important topics to be covered, including elements of the offence and possible defences if applicable.

Investigatively Important Topics

Identify topics needing to be explored for the investigation, including:

Circumstances of alleged offending including what happened immediately before, during and after the alleged offence.

All physical and verbal interactions between the witness and the alleged offender or anyone else.

Type and severity of alleged offence.

Elements of the offence and probable defences.

Identity and description of the suspect and other witnesses:

• If asking a witness to describe a person ask them the following question. “Describe

the person in as much detail as possible.”

• Explore the description topics in the order given by the witness.

• At the completion of the witnesses’ description you will need to cover off on areas

not addressed, for example, gender, race, age, height, build, hair (style, length, colour), skin colour, complexion, distinguishing features, clothing, carrying anything etc.

Cover ADVOKATE with witness if relevant.

Descriptions of the scene, PM and other items of interest.

ADVOKATE

ADVOKATE was created as a result of UK case law, R v TURNBALL 1977, in relation to mistaken identity. The guidelines set out the points significant witnesses need to cover when describing/identifying someone or something.

These points need to be covered for anything of significance, i.e. Suspect, Vehicle of Interest (VOI). It is recommended that ADVOKATE be used more than once during your interview to gain maximum detailed information.

A – Amount of time under observation.

D – Distance from the witnessed event.

V – Visibility at the time of the event.

O – Obstructions.

K – Known or seen before.

A – Any reason to remember.

T – Time since the incident.

E – Errors or discrepancies.

sections.h:
Build up a profile of the witness by gaining as much information as possible about:

Name and preferred name (For example, Matthew but prefers Matt).

Gender and sexuality if relevant.

Age and maturity.

Race, culture, religion and first language.

Any physical, intellectual, psychological or psychiatric impairment.

Relationship of witness to the alleged offender.

Domestic circumstances.

Current or previous contact with police.

Employment and routines.

Conduct check of police intelligence holdings (IMS, IDM etc.).

Also consider if you have identified factors affecting the vulnerability of the witness, ensure the witness is interviewed by a SCI or an interpreter or translator are used if required.

Witness: Emotional State

Consider the witness's emotional and physical state including:

Any trauma suffered.

Fears of intimidation.

Likely impact of recalling traumatic events on the witness's welfare.

Impact on behaviour of physical injuries, intoxication, tiredness and so on (delay the interview if appropriate).

Whether the witness is currently in a safe environment.

Type of evidence the witness is likely to give.

It is paramount you consider the witness's well-being and your investigative needs in this decision making.

Preparing the Interview Location

A quiet room that is free from distractions and interruptions allowing both you and the witness to concentrate.

Set up the interview room and arrange the seating.

Check equipment.

Make sure you have communication aids – including pen and paper for drawing sketch.

Consider implications if a support person or interpreter/translator is required.

Provide the witness with a drink of water and any available refreshments if required.

Preparing the Witness For Interview

The Engage and Explain interview phase may be immediately prior to the interview or on a separate occasion depending on the circumstances of the case. It can take place in person or over the telephone.

sections.h:
Engage the Witness

Establish rapport to put the witness at ease by:

Introducing yourself and any others present.

Asking the witness what they would like to be called.

Confirming the witness has a suitable amount of time for the interview.

Personalising the interview, i.e. treat the witness as an individual and talk to them in a manner and language they understand.

Discussing neutral topics.

Communicating empathy, i.e. addressing any concerns about events, the interview and the investigation.

Maintain impartiality to establish the foundation for a relationship of trust by:

• Keeping an open mind, i.e. don't pre-judge the witness.

• Using open questions and not interrupting - begin with a TEDS style question to

encourage the witness to provide sufficient information.

• Actively listening.

Explain Interview Procedures

Explain interview procedures including:

Reason(s) for the interview - do this in a way that makes the interview's purpose clear.

The interview structure - tell them they will be asked for their account and then you may ask questions to clarify their information.

Summarising:

• Allows the interviewer to check understanding of what has been said and in doing

so provides the interviewee with the opportunity to make any corrections.

• It shows you value what the interviewee tells you and encourages them to become

increasingly open with you.

• It assists you with encoding the information into your memory through rehearsal.

• It confirms the account and ownership of the information as correct by the

interviewee.

The process to be adopted - note taking and if you are not making eye contact it does not mean you are not listening.

They will need to concentrate to ensure they recall as much information as possible.

You will give them time to think to assist with the recall process.

Give an account of all they know in as much detail as possible.

Do not edit their account, let them tell you everything that comes to mind – including all the information they are not confident about or think may be trivial or partial, information they think you already know.

Take their time and set their own pace.

You want them to:

• Do most of the talking because they have information you want to know.

• Give you as much detail as possible and tell you if they don’t know something.

• Not be influenced by what they think you or others might want them to say.

• Correct you if they feel you misunderstood something they said.

sections.h:
When interviewing a witness the Free Recall Model should be used in the majority of occasions. A tier two interviewing officer may utilise the Cognitive Interviewing Model if the interviewer is of the view it would assist the witness with their recall and if the circumstances are appropriate (Cooperative, non-traumatised witness). The cognitive interview is a memory facilitator, with the interviewee being in control of the information flowing from the interview.

Free Recall Model

Step 1 Set the scene and initiate a free recall using an open question.

Step 2 Second recall if required. Request a sketch if relevant.

Step 3 Identify interviewee topics.

Step 4 Expand on topics, summarise and clarify.

Step 5 Identify and expand on interviewer topics (refer to your plan).

Step 6 Summarise, Clarify and prepare statement.

• Ensure your questions are relevant and have a purpose.

• Be mindful of the tone used in questioning.

• Always open with a TEDS question.

• Probe with TEDS and/or 5W&H questions.

• Use closed questions if required or as a last resort.

• At the end of the interview ask the witness if there is anything else relevant that has

not been discussed.

Cognitive Interview Model

The Cognitive Interview Model utilises the framework of the Free Recall Model. The cognitive interview primarily aims to place the witness back in the environment of the witnessed event. The process is undertaken to assist the witness to recall the event.

Cognitive interviewing should only be conducted on a cooperative non-traumatised witness by a tier two trained interviewer.

The instruction used, asks the interviewee to reconstruct in their mind the context, both physical (environmental) and personal (For example, how they felt at the time) of the witnessed event.

‘You witnessed an incident/event this morning, tell me everything you can, even the things you think are not important, and even if you can’t remember something completely’.

(PAUSE)

‘Everything that comes to mind, tell me in your own time and pace’.

Think of where you were. (PAUSE)

How are you feeling? (PAUSE)

What are you hearing? (PAUSE)

What can you smell? (PAUSE)

Think of all the people present. (PAUSE)

Think about all the objects there. Think of the layout of the (where the incident took place).

(PAUSE)

‘Get a really good picture in your mind and tell me everything you can remember without leaving anything out. All that pops into your mind, tell me’. (PAUSE) Note Taking

Notes of an interview are of immense value to the interviewer. They provide cues to the interviewer to use to steer the interview in a particular direction, which is of particular importance at the probing stage.

However, the notes should not be allowed to affect a free flowing interview that is already heading in the right direction. Nor should notes be taken with the intent to produce a verbatim account of what was said (That can be challenging, even for a professional stenographer).

Highly detailed note taking can focus the interviewer’s attention too sharply on the notes and not sharply enough on what the interviewee is saying. Where notes are concerned, in an interview context all that is required is a brief record, often just key words or topics the interviewer may want to investigate more fully.

Notes are also useful to the interviewer in terms of dividing the interviewee’s account into sections that the interviewer can manage more effectively, particularly in relation to scheduling sections for further development at the probing stage of the interview. It does not matter if the notes are illegible to other people; it is important the interviewer can read and use them as an aid to memory.

The notes should be clear enough so that the interviewer can obtain cues from them quickly and without interrupting the natural pace of the interview. There are a variety of different note taking methods, including, topic boxes, timelines, mind bubbles, bullet points etc. Notes are a tool to assist the interviewer effectively remember information supplied by the interviewee.

It does not matter what method is used by the interviewer so long as the interviewer is able to decipher the notes and record sufficient information to ensure the witness statement compiled after the interview is complete and accurate.

All notes made during an interview by the interviewer or the interviewee are disclosable and will be retained in the CRIME file.

Witness Sketch

To help the interviewee to communicate with you and to recall events, give them the opportunity to draw a sketch if relevant. Remember a picture speaks a thousand words. Any sketch produced should be signed, dated and given a title by the witness. It may later be produced as an exhibit. It will assist the witness and members of the jury should the matter come to trial.

Keep the sketch available for review and reference as required throughout the interview.

Any sketches made during an interview by the interviewer or the interviewee are disclosable and will be retained in the CRIME file.

CLOSURE

Closing Interviews

At the completion of the interview ensure the following points have been covered:

• Review information obtained and confirm all topics have been covered by referring to

your interview plan.

• Ask if the witness has any questions and answer them appropriately.

• Thank the witness for their time and effort.

• Request that if they recall further information about the event after the interview, to

contact you so an additional interview can be arranged.

• Prepare for future events (e.g. referral to support services, photographs, medical

examination, court or further police involvement with the witness).

• Provide them with a card including your name and contact details.

• Ensure victim support service details are provided to the witness.

EVALUATE

As soon as practical after the interview, consider:

• The impact of the information on the investigation.

• What evidence there is in relation to offences, elements of the offence and potential

defences.

• Descriptions of people, items and events that may be vital to the investigation, the

urgency and need for further enquiries.

Prior to asking the witness to sign their prepared statement ask a colleague or supervisor to proof read the statement to ensure all relevant information has been obtained and adequately covered.

Consider what follow-up action is required to prepare the witness for any future court proceedings. It may be advantageous to keep in regular contact with the witness especially as court proceedings approach.

Self- Evaluation

Self-evaluation is an important part of professional development. Through critical review of your performance you can identify areas of improvement that may lead to more professional collection of evidence.

Self-evaluate your own performance:

What did you do well?

What could you have done better?

What areas can you develop?

How will you acquire these skills?

ADVICE

For advice on investigative interviewing related matters contact the Detective Training School or the Investigative Standards Unit.

sections.h:
Anunga Rules

AD-01.00 Attachment B - Anunga Rules

Judgement Delivered By Chief Justice Forster

On 30 April, 1979, Mr. Justice FORSTER handed down his reasons for rejecting typewritten records of conversations in the matter of QUEEN v Angus ANUNGA, Sandy AJAX, Clancy AJAX, TJINGUNYA, Nari WHEELER and Frankie Miller JAGAMARA in the Northern Territory Supreme Court in August, 1975. (R v Anunga (1976) 11 ALR 412) Inter alia Mr. Justice FORSTER said:

"It seems to me now, however, to be important that the Court should put on record general guidelines for the conduct of Police Officers when interrogating Aboriginal persons.

I preface this statement of guidelines by pointing out that Aboriginal people often do not understand English very well and that, even if they do understand the words, they may not understand the concepts which English phrases and sentences express. Even with the use of interpreters this problem is by no means solved. Police and legal English sometimes is not translatable into the Aboriginal language at all and there are no separate Aboriginal words for some simple words like "in", "at", "on", "by", "with", or "over'', these being suffixes added to the word they qualify. Some words may translate literally into Aboriginal language but mean something different. "Did you go into his house?" means to an English-speaking person, "did you go into the building?", but to an Aboriginal it may also mean, "Did you go within the fence surrounding the house?" English concepts of time, number and distance are imperfectly understood if at all by Aboriginal people, many of the more primitive of whom cannot tell the time by a clock. One frequently hears the answer "long time", which depending on the context may be minutes, hours, days, weeks, or years. In case I may be misunderstood, I should also emphasise that I am not expressing the view that Aboriginal people are any less intelligent than white people but simply that their concepts of certain things and the terms in which they are expressed may be wholly different to those of white people.

Another matter which needs to be understood is that most Aboriginal people are basically courteous and polite and will answer questions by white people in the way in which they think the questioner wants. Even if they are not courteous and polite there is the same reaction when they are dealing with an authority figure such as a policeman. Indeed their action is probably a combination of natural politeness and their attitude to someone in authority. Some Aboriginal people find the standard caution quite bewildering, even if they understand that they do not have to answer questions, because if they do not have to answer questions, then why are the questions being asked?

Guidelines to be Followed when Interviewing Aboriginals Bearing in mind these preliminary observations which are based partly upon my own knowledge and partly by evidence I have heard in numerous cases I lay down the following guidelines. They apply, of course, to persons who are being questioned as suspects.

1. When an Aboriginal person is being interrogated as a suspect, unless he is as fluent

in English as the average white man of English descent, an interpreter able to interpret in and from the Aboriginal person's language should be present, and his assistance should be utilised whenever necessary to ensure complete and mutual understanding.

2. When an Aboriginal is being interrogated it is desirable where practicable that a

"prisoner's friend" (who may also be the interpreter) be present. The "prisoner's friend', should be someone in whom the Aboriginal has apparent confidence. He may be a mission or settlement superintendent or a member of the staff of one of these institutions who knows and is known to the Aboriginal. He may be a station owner, manager or overseer or an officer from the Department of Aboriginal Affairs. The combinations of persons and situations are variable and the categories of persons I have mentioned are not exclusive. The important thing is that the "prisoner's friend', be someone in whom the Aboriginal has confidence, by whom he will feel supported.

3. Great care should be taken in administering the caution when it is appropriate to do

so. It is simply not adequate to administer it in the usual terms and say, "Do you understand that?" or "Do you understand you do not have to answer questions?"

Interrogating Police Officers, having explained the caution in simple terms, should ask the Aboriginal to tell them what is meant by the caution, phrase by phrase, and should not proceed with the interrogation until it is clear the Aboriginal has apparent understanding of his right to remain silent. Most experienced police officers in the Territory already do this. The problem of the caution is a difficult one but the presence of a "prisoner's friend" or interpreter and adequate and simple questioning about the caution should go a long way towards solving it.

4. Great care should be taken in formulating questions so that so far as possible the

answer which is wanted or expected is not suggested in any way. Anything in the nature of cross-examination should be scrupulously avoided as answers to it have no probative value. It should be borne in mind that it is not only the wording of the question, which may suggest the answer, but also the manner and tone of voice which are used.

5. Even when an apparent frank and free confession has been obtained relating to the

commission of an offence, police should continue to investigate the matter in an endeavour to obtain proof of the commission of the offence from other sources.

Failure to do this, among other things, led to the rejection of confessional records of interview in the cases of Nari WHEELER and Frank JAGAMARA.

6. Because Aboriginal people are often nervous and ill at ease in the presence of white

authority figures like policemen, it is particularly important that they be offered a meal, if they are being interviewed in a Police Station, or in the company of Police or in custody when a meal time arrives. They should always be offered a drink of water.

They should be asked if they wish to use the lavatory if they are in the company of Police or under arrest.

7. It is particularly important that Aboriginal and other people are not interrogated when

they are disabled by illness or drunkenness or tiredness. Admissions so gained will probably be rejected by a Court. Interrogation should not continue for an unreasonably long time.

8. Should an Aboriginal seek legal assistance reasonable steps should be taken to

obtain such assistance. If an Aboriginal states he does not wish to answer further questions or any questions the interrogation should not continue.

9. When it is necessary to remove clothing for forensic examination or for the purposes

of medical examination, steps must be taken forthwith to supply substitute clothing."

(R v Anunga (1976) 11 ALR 412) Inter alia Mr. Justice FORSTER said: "It seems to me now, however, to be important that the Court should put on record general guidelines for the conduct of Police Officers when interrogating Aboriginal persons. I preface this statement of guidelines by pointing out that Aboriginal people often do not understand English very well and that, even if they do understand the words, they may not understand the concepts which English phrases and sentences express. Even with the use of interpreters this problem is by no means solved. Police and legal English sometimes is not translatable into the Aboriginal language at all and there are no separate Aboriginal words for some simple words like "in", "at", "on", "by", "with", or "over'', these being suffixes added to the word they qualify. Some words may translate literally into Aboriginal language but mean something different. "Did you go into his house?" means to an English-speaking person, "did you go into the building?", but to an Aboriginal it may also mean, "Did you go within the fence surrounding the house?" English concepts of time, number and distance are imperfectly understood if at all by Aboriginal people, many of the more primitive of whom cannot tell the time by a clock. One frequently hears the answer "long time", which depending on the context may be minutes, hours, days, weeks, or years. In case I may be misunderstood, I should also emphasise that I am not expressing the view that Aboriginal people are any less intelligent than white people but simply that their concepts of certain things and the terms in which they are expressed may be wholly different to those of white people. Another matter which needs to be understood is that most Aboriginal people are basically courteous and polite and will answer questions by white people in the way in which they think the questioner wants. Even if they are not courteous and polite there is the same reaction when they are dealing with an authority figure such as a policeman. Indeed their action is probably a combination of natural politeness and their attitude to someone in authority. Some Aboriginal people find the standard caution quite bewildering, even if they understand that they do not have to answer questions, because if they do not have to answer questions, then why are the questions being asked? Guidelines to be Followed when Interviewing Aboriginals Bearing in mind these preliminary observations which are based partly upon my own knowledge and partly by evidence I have heard in numerous cases I lay down the following guidelines. They apply, of course, to persons who are being questioned as suspects. 1. When an Aboriginal person is being interrogated as a suspect, unless he is as fluent in English as the average white man of English descent, an interpreter able to interpret in and from the Aboriginal person's language should be present, and his assistance should be utilised whenever necessary to ensure complete and mutual understanding. 2. When an Aboriginal is being interrogated it is desirable where practicable that a "prisoner's friend" (who may also be the interpreter) be present. The "prisoner's friend', should be someone in whom the Aboriginal has apparent confidence. He may be a mission or settlement superintendent or a member of the staff of one of these institutions who knows and is known to the Aboriginal. He may be a station owner, manager or overseer or an officer from the Department of Aboriginal Affairs. The combinations of persons and situations are variable and the categories of persons I have mentioned are not exclusive. The important thing is that the "prisoner's friend', be someone in whom the Aboriginal has confidence, by whom he will feel supported. 3. Great care should be taken in administering the caution when it is appropriate to do so. It is simply not adequate to administer it in the usual terms and say, "Do you understand that?" or "Do you understand you do not have to answer questions?" Interrogating Police Officers, having explained the caution in simple terms, should ask the Aboriginal to tell them what is meant by the caution, phrase by phrase, and should not proceed with the interrogation until it is clear the Aboriginal has apparent understanding of his right to remain silent. Most experienced police officers in the Territory already do this. The problem of the caution is a difficult one but the presence of a "prisoner's friend" or interpreter and adequate and simple questioning about the caution should go a long way towards solving it. 4. Great care should be taken in formulating questions so that so far as possible the answer which is wanted or expected is not suggested in any way. Anything in the nature of cross-examination should be scrupulously avoided as answers to it have no probative value. It should be borne in mind that it is not only the wording of the question, which may suggest the answer, but also the manner and tone of voice which are used. 5. Even when an apparent frank and free confession has been obtained relating to the commission of an offence, police should continue to investigate the matter in an endeavour to obtain proof of the commission of the offence from other sources. Failure to do this, among other things, led to the rejection of confessional records of interview in the cases of Nari WHEELER and Frank JAGAMARA. 6. Because Aboriginal people are often nervous and ill at ease in the presence of white authority figures like policemen, it is particularly important that they be offered a meal, if they are being interviewed in a Police Station, or in the company of Police or in custody when a meal time arrives. They should always be offered a drink of water. They should be asked if they wish to use the lavatory if they are in the company of Police or under arrest. 7. It is particularly important that Aboriginal and other people are not interrogated when they are disabled by illness or drunkenness or tiredness. Admissions so gained will probably be rejected by a Court. Interrogation should not continue for an unreasonably long time. 8. Should an Aboriginal seek legal assistance reasonable steps should be taken to obtain such assistance. If an Aboriginal states he does not wish to answer further questions or any questions the interrogation should not continue. 9. When it is necessary to remove clothing for forensic examination or for the purposes of medical examination, steps must be taken forthwith to supply substitute clothing."

sections.h:
Section 10 Criminal Investigation Act 2006

Interpreters

Criminal Investigation Act (CIA) 2006 s.10 - Informing people who do not understand English.

If under this Act (CIA) an officer is required to inform a person about any matter and the person is for any reason unable to understand or communicate in spoken English sufficiently, the officer must, if it is practical to do so in the circumstances, use an interpreter or other qualified person or other means to inform the person about the matter.

Identify the country they come from and the exact language they speak (For example, a Chinese person may speak Mandarin or Cantonese.;

Arrange for an interpreter using the contracted interpreting service. Provide the interpreter with an outline of the incident and the reason for the interview.

Ascertain whether the interpreter is an appropriate person to assist. They must be able to write and speak the suspect or witness’s language fluently and be impartial and independent to the investigation. This means they must have no prior knowledge of the suspect or witnesses involved.

If they know any of the parties involved in the investigation (including the suspect), they should only be used in exceptional circumstances, i.e. no one else is available and the interview cannot be delayed. The extent of the connection should also be taken into account when making this decision. If you use an interpreter who knows the parties involved, make sure you keep a record of your rationale.

Record the interpreter’s qualifications and contact details or those of their organisation.

Prepare for the interview in the usual manner.

When the interpreter arrives:

• Allow the interpreter the opportunity to brief both you and the witness on their

professional role and how they will conduct themselves;

• If necessary, inform the interpreter their role is to interpret your questions and the

witness’s answers back to you;

• Their interpretation should be as direct as possible in 'first person'. They should not

enter into general discussion with the witness.

Make sure the interpreter understands the Section 28 CIA rights. Instruct them to clearly explain these to the witness to ensure the witness understands.

Consider seeking advice from the interpreter about any communication issues that may arise.

Consider the interpreter's needs for breaks during the interview.

Using the interpreter, complete the usual Engage and Explain process as you would for any other witness. Where appropriate, inform the witness of their rights with the aid of the interpreter.

While video recording the interview:

• Ask the interpreter to introduce themselves, explain their role, experience and

qualifications. (They may later be required to give evidence in respect of their knowledge and experience).

• Explain the interpreter's role as detailed above.

• Ensure the interpreter is clearly visible during the interview.

Do not speak in segments. Let the interpreter control the length of each segment and signal to you when to stop.

Complete the account phase using an appropriate interview model. Your questions are interpreted to the witness in their language and their answers interpreted back to you.

For safety and impartiality reasons make sure the interpreter leaves the room with you if you take any interview breaks.

Complete the closure process as usual.

Before the interpreter leaves, ensure you have their full contact details (or that of their agency).

sections.h:
Interviewee’s account:

• Tell me your involvement in (state the offence)

• ADMITS OFFENCE -Tell me what happened

• DENIES OFFENCE -Tell me your movements between the time / date surrounding the offence

1) Obtain Suspects (or interviewee’s) Account.

2) Identify Topic/s.

3) Summarise the suspect’s (or interviewee’s) initial account.

4) Expand Topic/s (TEDS, 5W & 1H- Who, What, When, Where, Why & How).

5) Refer to second interviewing officer at the end of each topic.

6) Summarise each topic before moving to next topic.

7) Move to next topic and do steps 4, 5, 6 and 7 until all of the suspects account has been covered.

Investigator’s agenda:

• Question the interviewee on investigative areas not raised by interviewee such as elements of offence, defences,

prior knowledge etc.

• Have the interviewee explain their knowledge of the relevant exhibits.

• Probe interviewee’s responses using appropriate questioning techniques but do not challenge with contradictory

evidence.

Investigative Standards Unit

WA Police Force

Investigator’s agenda (cont.):

• Allow corroborator to ask questions to clarify on each topic before moving to next topic.

• Summarise each topic before moving to next topic.

Challenge Phase:

• Challenge inconsistencies in the interviewee’s account and those arising from evidence gathered through other

sources (eg: physical evidence, witness accounts).

• Challenges should be presented in a manner that is non-confrontational but seeking clarification from the

interviewee. (eg “You have said….., we know / have…….explain that to me?”)

• Challenges should be presented from weakest to strongest asking for an explanation on each one before moving

to the next.

Do not express personal opinions regarding the truthfulness of the interviewee’s account.

sections.h:
Provide arrested suspects rights

Notified Of Afforded

• The offence for which they have been arrested. • Any necessary medical treatment.

• Caution before interviewing. • Reasonable degree of privacy from mass media.

• Reasonable opportunity to communicate with a lawyer. • If they are unable to understand spoken English

• Reasonable opportunity to communicate with a friend sufficiently, not to be interviewed until the services

or family member to advise them of their whereabouts. of an interpreter are available.

Ask suspect if they wish to exercise any of the above rights

• You have the right to speak to a lawyer. Do you wish to exercise that right?

• You have the right to speak with a friend or relative to advise them of your whereabouts. Do you wish to exercise that right?

If the suspect wishes to contact a lawyer, follow this procedure:

• Turn off BWC to allow private communication between the suspect and their legal representative.

• Prioritise the suspect’s security by ensuring they do not access any area unsupervised.

• Maintain visual oversight from an appropriate vantage point while still preserving the privacy of the conversation.

• If the suspect is advised by a legal representative not to participate in an interview and this is confirmed by the suspect, the

interview should not proceed.

• If the suspect elects to continue the interview after speaking with a lawyer, confirm on BWC that legal advice has been

obtained, re-caution and ensure suspect is happy to continue with interview.
